1→# AI-Generated TypeScript Code: Common Flaws, Mistakes, and Anti-Patterns 2→ 3→## Comprehensive Research Findings 4→ 5→--- 6→ 7→## 1. TYPE SAFETY ISSUES 8→ 9→### 1.1 Abuse of `any` Type 10→ 11→**Description:** When AI models cannot determine the correct type, they default to `any`, effectively disabling TypeScript's type system. This has been called "AI catnip" -- agents reach for `any` whenever they encounter type complexity. 12→ 13→**Bad code:** 14→```typescript 15→function processUser(data: any) { 16→ const email = data.user.email; 17→ return email.toLowerCase(); 18→} 19→ 20→function handleResponse(response: any) { 21→ return response.data.items.map((item: any) => item.name); 22→} 23→``` 24→ 25→**Good code:** 26→```typescript 27→interface UserData { 28→ user: { 29→ email: string; 30→ }; 31→} 32→ 33→function processUser(data: UserData): string { 34→ return data.user.email.toLowerCase(); 35→} 36→ 37→interface ApiResponse { 38→ data: { 39→ items: T[]; 40→ }; 41→} 42→ 43→function handleResponse(response: ApiResponse<{ name: string }>): string[] { 44→ return response.data.items.map((item) => item.name); 45→} 46→``` 47→ 48→**Why AI does this:** Models optimize for code that compiles and runs. Using `any` is the fastest path to eliminating type errors without understanding the domain. 49→ 50→**Impact:** Runtime crashes from accessing properties on undefined/null, loss of IDE autocomplete, defeats the purpose of TypeScript entirely. Errors surface in production instead of at compile time. 51→ 52→**Detection:** ESLint rule `@typescript-eslint/no-explicit-any`. TypeScript `strict: true` in tsconfig.json. 53→ 54→**Sources:** 55→- [Lint Against the Machine: AI Coding Agent Anti-Patterns](https://medium.com/@montes.makes/lint-against-the-machine-a-field-guide-to-catching-ai-coding-agent-anti-patterns-3c4ef7baeb9e) 56→- [5 Things AI Can't Do in TypeScript](https://dev.to/devunionx/5-things-ai-cant-do-even-in-typescript-1ahm) 57→ 58→--- 59→ 60→### 1.2 Unsafe Type Assertions (`as` / `as unknown as`) 61→ 62→**Description:** AI uses type assertions to silence the compiler rather than fixing the underlying type mismatch. The double assertion pattern (`as unknown as X`) is particularly dangerous. 63→ 64→**Bad code:** 65→```typescript 66→const user = {} as User; // Missing all required fields 67→console.log(user.profile.bio); // Runtime crash 68→ 69→const config = response.data as unknown as AppConfig; // Double assertion bypasses all checks 70→``` 71→ 72→**Good code:** 73→```typescript 74→import { z } from 'zod'; 75→ 76→const userSchema = z.object({ 77→ id: z.number(), 78→ email: z.string(), 79→ profile: z.object({ bio: z.string() }).optional(), 80→}); 81→ 82→const user = userSchema.parse(response.data); 83→console.log(user.profile?.bio); 84→``` 85→ 86→**Why AI does this:** Assertions make code "compile" immediately. AI models optimize for functional output and don't understand that `as` doesn't perform runtime validation. 87→ 88→**Impact:** Type assertions are a lie to the compiler. Missing required properties won't be flagged, leading to runtime crashes that TypeScript was designed to prevent. 89→ 90→**Detection:** ESLint rule `@typescript-eslint/consistent-type-assertions` with `assertionStyle: 'never'` or strict configurations. Use runtime validation libraries (Zod, io-ts) for external data. 91→ 92→**Sources:** 93→- [Safe TypeScript Type Casting](https://medium.com/@AlexanderObregon/safe-typescript-type-casting-without-surprises-a1afe26a36a1) 94→- [Understanding "as unknown as" in TypeScript](https://www.omarileon.me/blog/as-unknown-as-typescript) 95→ 96→--- 97→ 98→### 1.3 Overcomplicated or Incorrect Generics 99→ 100→**Description:** AI either oversimplifies generics (defaulting to `any`/`unknown`) or overcomplicates them with deeply nested conditional types that are unreadable and sometimes incorrect. 101→ 102→**Bad code (overcomplicated):** 103→```typescript 104→// AI-generated type spaghetti 105→type MappedParameterTypes

= P extends { type: "enum"; enum: infer E } 106→ ? E extends readonly string[] 107→ ? E[number] 108→ : string // Falls back to string, losing precision 109→ : P extends { type: "object[]"; attributes?: never } 110→ ? any[] // Falls back to any[] 111→ : P extends { type: "object[]"; attributes: infer A } 112→ ? MappedParameterTypes[] 113→ : never; 114→``` 115→ 116→**Bad code (oversimplified):** 117→```typescript 118→function merge(a: T, b: T): T { 119→ return { ...a, ...b }; // Doesn't actually guarantee T shape 120→} 121→``` 122→ 123→**Good code:** 124→```typescript 125→function merge>(a: T, b: Partial): T { 126→ return { ...a, ...b }; 127→} 128→``` 129→ 130→**Why AI does this:** AI repeats patterns from training data without understanding type resolution logic. Deep generic chains are pattern-matched, not reasoned about. Nearly 40% of developers reported confusion when debugging overly generic AI implementations. 131→ 132→**Impact:** Overcomplicated types slow the TypeScript compiler, confuse maintainers, and may silently fall through to `any`. Incorrect constraints lose type safety or reject valid inputs. 133→ 134→**Detection:** Code review for generic depth > 3 levels. Cognitive complexity analysis. TypeScript `--noUncheckedIndexedAccess` flag. 135→ 136→**Sources:** 137→- [Trying to Understand Copilot's Type Spaghetti](https://rtpg.co/2024/03/07/parsing-copilots-type-spaghetti/) 138→- [5 Things AI Can't Do in TypeScript](https://dev.to/devunionx/5-things-ai-cant-do-even-in-typescript-1ahm) 139→ 140→--- 141→ 142→### 1.4 Missing Strict TypeScript Configuration 143→ 144→**Description:** AI-generated projects often use loose tsconfig settings, or AI code assumes non-strict mode behavior. 145→ 146→**Bad tsconfig.json:** 147→```json 148→{ 149→ "compilerOptions": { 150→ "strict": false, 151→ "noImplicitAny": false, 152→ "strictNullChecks": false 153→ } 154→} 155→``` 156→ 157→**Good tsconfig.json:** 158→```json 159→{ 160→ "compilerOptions": { 161→ "strict": true, 162→ "noUncheckedIndexedAccess": true, 163→ "noImplicitReturns": true, 164→ "noFallthroughCasesInSwitch": true, 165→ "forceConsistentCasingInFileNames": true 166→ } 167→} 168→``` 169→ 170→**Why AI does this:** Training data includes both strict and non-strict projects. AI chooses whatever compiles first. 171→ 172→**Impact:** Without strict mode, TypeScript misses null/undefined errors, implicit any, and other class of bugs that strict mode catches. 173→ 174→**Detection:** Check tsconfig.json for `"strict": true`. Use `@typescript-eslint/strict-type-checked` config. 175→ 176→--- 177→ 178→### 1.5 Hallucinated Properties and Interfaces 179→ 180→**Description:** AI generates properties on interfaces that don't exist in the actual API or data model, or invents interface shapes that don't match real library types. 181→ 182→**Bad code:** 183→```typescript 184→interface User { 185→ name: string; 186→ email: string; 187→} 188→ 189→const user: User = { 190→ name: 'John', 191→ email: 'john@example.com', 192→ phoneNumber: '555-1234', // Property doesn't exist on User 193→}; 194→``` 195→ 196→**Why AI does this:** Models predict plausible property names based on context patterns. "Most production outages from AI-generated code trace back to data model mismatches that passed code review." 197→ 198→**Impact:** TypeScript catches some of these at compile time (excess property checks), but type assertions bypass this protection. Runtime crashes when accessing non-existent properties. 199→ 200→**Detection:** TypeScript strict mode, runtime validation with Zod/io-ts, integration tests against real APIs. 201→ 202→**Sources:** 203→- [Debugging AI-Generated Code: 8 Failure Patterns](https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/debugging-ai-generated-code-8-failure-patterns-and-fixes) 204→- [Common Bugs in AI-Generated Code](https://www.ranger.net/post/common-bugs-ai-generated-code-fixes) 205→ 206→--- 207→ 208→## 2. RUNTIME ERRORS FROM INCORRECT ASSUMPTIONS 209→ 210→### 2.1 Missing Null/Undefined Checks 211→ 212→**Description:** AI assumes happy-path data shapes and fails to handle null, undefined, or missing nested properties. 213→ 214→**Bad code:** 215→```typescript 216→function processUser(data: any) { 217→ const email = data.user.email; // Crashes if data.user is null/undefined 218→ return email.toLowerCase(); // Crashes if email is null/undefined 219→} 220→``` 221→ 222→**Good code:** 223→```typescript 224→function processUser(data: unknown): string | null { 225→ if ( 226→ typeof data === 'object' && data !== null && 227→ 'user' in data && typeof (data as any).user === 'object' && 228→ (data as any).user !== null && 229→ 'email' in (data as any).user && 230→ typeof (data as any).user.email === 'string' 231→ ) { 232→ return (data as { user: { email: string } }).user.email.toLowerCase(); 233→ } 234→ return null; 235→} 236→ 237→// Or more practically with optional chaining: 238→function processUser(data: { user?: { email?: string } }): string | null { 239→ return data?.user?.email?.toLowerCase() ?? null; 240→} 241→``` 242→ 243→**Why AI does this:** AI models focus on making functional code that works with expected inputs. Training data contains many examples of "happy path" code. 244→ 245→**Impact:** Runtime TypeError crashes. "Silent logic failures make up 60% of faults" in AI-generated code. 246→ 247→**Detection:** TypeScript `strictNullChecks: true`. ESLint rule `@typescript-eslint/no-non-null-assertion`. 248→ 249→**Sources:** 250→- [Common Bugs in AI-Generated Code](https://www.ranger.net/post/common-bugs-ai-generated-code-fixes) 251→- [Debugging AI-Generated Code](https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/debugging-ai-generated-code-8-failure-patterns-and-fixes) 252→ 253→--- 254→ 255→### 2.2 Off-by-One Errors and Boundary Conditions 256→ 257→**Description:** AI frequently produces off-by-one errors in loops and fails to handle edge cases like empty arrays, zero-length strings, or boundary values. 258→ 259→**Bad code:** 260→```typescript 261→function findIndex(arr: number[], target: number): number { 262→ for (let i = 0; i <= arr.length; i++) { // Off-by-one: should be < 263→ if (arr[i] === target) return i; 264→ } 265→ return -1; 266→} 267→ 268→function getFirst(arr: T[]): T { 269→ return arr[0]; // No bounds check; returns undefined for empty array 270→} 271→``` 272→ 273→**Good code:** 274→```typescript 275→function findIndex(arr: number[], target: number): number { 276→ for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) { 277→ if (arr[i] === target) return i; 278→ } 279→ return -1; 280→} 281→ 282→function getFirst(arr: T[]): T | undefined { 283→ return arr.length > 0 ? arr[0] : undefined; 284→} 285→``` 286→ 287→**Why AI does this:** Boundary conditions are edge cases that appear infrequently in training data. AI prioritizes the common case. 288→ 289→**Impact:** Array index out of bounds, accessing undefined values, infinite loops. 290→ 291→**Detection:** Unit tests with empty arrays, null values, zero, negative numbers, maximum integers. TypeScript `noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true`. 292→ 293→--- 294→ 295→### 2.3 Data Model Mismatches with External APIs 296→ 297→**Description:** AI assumes API response shapes that don't match reality, or generates TypeScript interfaces that diverge from actual backend schemas. 298→ 299→**Bad code:** 300→```typescript 301→interface User { 302→ id: number; 303→ email: string; 304→ profile: { bio: string }; 305→} 306→ 307→// Assumes shape without validation 308→const user: User = apiResponse; 309→console.log(user.profile.bio); // Crashes if profile is null or structure differs 310→``` 311→ 312→**Good code:** 313→```typescript 314→import { z } from 'zod'; 315→ 316→const userSchema = z.object({ 317→ id: z.number(), 318→ email: z.string(), 319→ profile: z.object({ bio: z.string() }).optional(), 320→}); 321→ 322→type User = z.infer; 323→ 324→const result = userSchema.safeParse(apiResponse); 325→if (result.success) { 326→ console.log(result.data.profile?.bio); 327→} else { 328→ console.error('Invalid API response:', result.error); 329→} 330→``` 331→ 332→**Why AI does this:** AI generates plausible-looking interfaces from context but cannot verify them against the actual API. It "cannot make such strategic choices based on team size, deployment complexity, or operational needs." 333→ 334→**Impact:** "Most production outages from AI-generated code trace back to data model mismatches." Silent failures where data is coerced incorrectly. 335→ 336→**Detection:** Runtime validation (Zod, io-ts, ajv). Contract testing. Integration tests against real APIs. 337→ 338→**Sources:** 339→- [Debugging AI-Generated Code: 8 Failure Patterns](https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/debugging-ai-generated-code-8-failure-patterns-and-fixes) 340→ 341→--- 342→ 343→## 3. SECURITY VULNERABILITIES 344→ 345→### 3.1 SQL Injection via String Interpolation 346→ 347→**Description:** AI frequently uses template literals or string concatenation to build SQL queries with user input, creating injection vulnerabilities. 348→ 349→**Bad code:** 350→```typescript 351→const query = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = '${userEmail}'`; 352→db.query(query); 353→ 354→// Or with template literal 355→const query = `SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ${userId}`; 356→``` 357→ 358→**Good code:** 359→```typescript 360→// Parameterized query 361→const query = 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = $1'; 362→db.query(query, [userEmail]); 363→ 364→// Or with an ORM 365→const user = await prisma.user.findUnique({ 366→ where: { email: userEmail }, 367→}); 368→``` 369→ 370→**Why AI does this:** Training data contains many examples of string interpolation. AI optimizes for readability and "making code work, not making code secure." A 2025 study found 45% of AI-generated code contains vulnerabilities. 371→ 372→**Impact:** Full database compromise. Data exfiltration. Authentication bypass. 373→ 374→**Detection:** CodeQL, Semgrep, ESLint `no-sql-injection` rules. SAST tools in CI/CD. 375→ 376→**Sources:** 377→- [Security Vulnerabilities in AI-Generated Code](https://www.endorlabs.com/learn/the-most-common-security-vulnerabilities-in-ai-generated-code) 378→- [OpenSSF Security Guide for AI Code Assistants](https://best.openssf.org/Security-Focused-Guide-for-AI-Code-Assistant-Instructions) 379→ 380→--- 381→ 382→### 3.2 Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via innerHTML 383→ 384→**Description:** AI uses `innerHTML` or `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` with user-supplied content without sanitization. 385→ 386→**Bad code:** 387→```typescript 388→// Vanilla JS/TS 389→element.innerHTML = userInput; 390→ 391→// React 392→function Comment({ text }: { text: string }) { 393→ return

; 394→} 395→``` 396→ 397→**Good code:** 398→```typescript 399→// Vanilla JS/TS 400→element.textContent = userInput; 401→ 402→// React - default behavior is safe 403→function Comment({ text }: { text: string }) { 404→ return
{text}
; // React auto-escapes 405→} 406→ 407→// If HTML is needed, sanitize first 408→import DOMPurify from 'dompurify'; 409→function Comment({ html }: { html: string }) { 410→ return
; 411→} 412→``` 413→ 414→**Why AI does this:** AI may generate `innerHTML` when asked to render dynamic content. It doesn't reason about trust boundaries. 415→ 416→**Impact:** Stored/reflected XSS, session hijacking, credential theft. 417→ 418→**Detection:** ESLint `no-inner-html` rule. React ESLint plugin flags `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`. Content Security Policy headers. 419→ 420→**Sources:** 421→- [TypeScript XSS Guide](https://www.stackhawk.com/blog/typescript-xss-guide-examples-and-prevention/) 422→- [OpenSSF Security Guide](https://best.openssf.org/Security-Focused-Guide-for-AI-Code-Assistant-Instructions) 423→ 424→--- 425→ 426→### 3.3 Command Injection 427→ 428→**Description:** AI uses `child_process.exec` with unsanitized user input to execute shell commands. 429→ 430→**Bad code:** 431→```typescript 432→import { exec } from 'child_process'; 433→ 434→function convertFile(filename: string) { 435→ exec(`convert ${filename} output.png`); // Shell injection 436→} 437→``` 438→ 439→**Good code:** 440→```typescript 441→import { execFile } from 'child_process'; 442→ 443→function convertFile(filename: string) { 444→ execFile('convert', [filename, 'output.png']); // Array args, no shell 445→} 446→``` 447→ 448→**Why AI does this:** `exec` with template strings is the simplest pattern. AI rarely considers adversarial inputs. 449→ 450→**Impact:** Remote code execution. Complete server compromise. 451→ 452→**Detection:** ESLint `security/detect-child-process` rule. SAST tools. Code review. 453→ 454→--- 455→ 456→### 3.4 Hardcoded Secrets and Credentials 457→ 458→**Description:** AI embeds API keys, passwords, and tokens directly in source code. 459→ 460→**Bad code:** 461→```typescript 462→const API_KEY = 'sk-1234567890abcdef'; 463→const client = new ApiClient({ apiKey: API_KEY }); 464→ 465→// Or in config files 466→const dbConfig = { 467→ host: 'production-db.example.com', 468→ password: 'super_secret_password', 469→}; 470→``` 471→ 472→**Good code:** 473→```typescript 474→function getApiClient(): ApiClient { 475→ const apiKey = process.env.API_KEY; 476→ if (!apiKey) { 477→ throw new Error('API_KEY environment variable is required'); 478→ } 479→ return new ApiClient({ apiKey }); 480→} 481→``` 482→ 483→**Why AI does this:** Training examples often include placeholder credentials. AI fills in realistic-looking values. 484→ 485→**Impact:** Credential exposure in version control. Account takeover. 486→ 487→**Detection:** git-secrets, truffleHog, GitHub secret scanning, ESLint `no-hardcoded-credentials` rules. 488→ 489→**Sources:** 490→- [OpenSSF Security Guide](https://best.openssf.org/Security-Focused-Guide-for-AI-Code-Assistant-Instructions) 491→- [Security Vulnerabilities in AI-Generated Code](https://www.endorlabs.com/learn/the-most-common-security-vulnerabilities-in-ai-generated-code) 492→ 493→--- 494→ 495→### 3.5 Prototype Pollution 496→ 497→**Description:** AI generates recursive merge functions or uses unsafe object spread patterns that are vulnerable to `__proto__` pollution. 498→ 499→**Bad code:** 500→```typescript 501→function deepMerge(target: any, source: any): any { 502→ for (const key in source) { 503→ if (typeof source[key] === 'object' && source[key] !== null) { 504→ target[key] = deepMerge(target[key] || {}, source[key]); 505→ } else { 506→ target[key] = source[key]; // __proto__ can be polluted 507→ } 508→ } 509→ return target; 510→} 511→ 512→// User input: { "__proto__": { "isAdmin": true } } 513→``` 514→ 515→**Good code:** 516→```typescript 517→function deepMerge>( 518→ target: T, 519→ source: Partial 520→): T { 521→ const result = { ...target }; 522→ for (const key of Object.keys(source)) { 523→ if (key === '__proto__' || key === 'constructor' || key === 'prototype') { 524→ continue; // Skip dangerous keys 525→ } 526→ if ( 527→ typeof source[key as keyof T] === 'object' && 528→ source[key as keyof T] !== null && 529→ !Array.isArray(source[key as keyof T]) 530→ ) { 531→ result[key as keyof T] = deepMerge( 532→ result[key as keyof T] as any, 533→ source[key as keyof T] as any 534→ ); 535→ } else { 536→ result[key as keyof T] = source[key as keyof T] as T[keyof T]; 537→ } 538→ } 539→ return result; 540→} 541→ 542→// Or preferably: use a well-tested library like lodash.merge or structuredClone 543→``` 544→ 545→**Why AI does this:** Recursive merge is a common pattern in training data, and most examples don't include prototype pollution guards. 546→ 547→**Impact:** Property injection on all objects, leading to authentication bypass, DoS, or RCE. 548→ 549→**Detection:** ESLint `security/detect-object-injection` rule. Snyk, npm audit. Use `Object.create(null)` for dictionaries. 550→ 551→**Sources:** 552→- [OWASP Prototype Pollution Prevention](https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Prototype_Pollution_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html) 553→- [Prototype Pollution in TypeScript](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/28596) 554→ 555→--- 556→ 557→### 3.6 Missing Input Validation 558→ 559→**Description:** AI frequently omits input validation entirely unless explicitly prompted. Even when prompted, validation may be inconsistent or simplistic. 560→ 561→**Bad code:** 562→```typescript 563→app.post('/api/users', async (req, res) => { 564→ const { email, name, age } = req.body; // No validation 565→ await db.users.create({ email, name, age }); 566→ res.json({ success: true }); 567→}); 568→``` 569→ 570→**Good code:** 571→```typescript 572→import { z } from 'zod'; 573→ 574→const createUserSchema = z.object({ 575→ email: z.string().email().max(255), 576→ name: z.string().min(1).max(100), 577→ age: z.number().int().min(0).max(150), 578→}); 579→ 580→app.post('/api/users', async (req, res) => { 581→ const result = createUserSchema.safeParse(req.body); 582→ if (!result.success) { 583→ return res.status(400).json({ errors: result.error.flatten() }); 584→ } 585→ await db.users.create(result.data); 586→ res.json({ success: true }); 587→}); 588→``` 589→ 590→**Why AI does this:** "AI-generated code frequently omits input validation unless explicitly prompted to include it." 591→ 592→**Impact:** Injection attacks, data corruption, unexpected runtime errors. 593→ 594→**Detection:** SAST tools. API testing. Code review checklist for all external inputs. 595→ 596→--- 597→ 598→## 4. PERFORMANCE ANTI-PATTERNS 599→ 600→### 4.1 O(n^2) Algorithms Where O(n) Suffices 601→ 602→**Description:** AI generates nested loops and string concatenation patterns that become performance bottlenecks at scale. GPT-4 generated code has been measured running 3x slower than human solutions. 603→ 604→**Bad code:** 605→```typescript 606→// String concatenation in loop - O(n^2) 607→let result = ''; 608→for (const item of largeArray) { 609→ result += item + ', '; 610→} 611→ 612→// Nested loop for lookup - O(n^2) 613→function findCommon(arr1: number[], arr2: number[]): number[] { 614→ return arr1.filter(item => arr2.includes(item)); 615→} 616→``` 617→ 618→**Good code:** 619→```typescript 620→// Array join - O(n) 621→const result = largeArray.join(', '); 622→ 623→// Set lookup - O(n) 624→function findCommon(arr1: number[], arr2: number[]): number[] { 625→ const set2 = new Set(arr2); 626→ return arr1.filter(item => set2.has(item)); 627→} 628→``` 629→ 630→**Why AI does this:** Development test datasets are too small to expose algorithmic complexity problems. The naive approach is the most common in training data. 631→ 632→**Impact:** Timeouts and degraded performance under production load. Memory pressure from unnecessary allocations. 633→ 634→**Detection:** Performance profiling (Clinic.js, Node.js profiler). Big-O analysis during code review. Benchmark tests. 635→ 636→**Sources:** 637→- [Debugging AI-Generated Code](https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/debugging-ai-generated-code-8-failure-patterns-and-fixes) 638→- [Common Bugs in AI-Generated Code](https://www.ranger.net/post/common-bugs-ai-generated-code-fixes) 639→ 640→--- 641→ 642→### 4.2 Missing Event Listener Cleanup / Memory Leaks 643→ 644→**Description:** AI generates event listeners without corresponding cleanup, especially in React components, leading to memory leaks. 645→ 646→**Bad code:** 647→```typescript 648→// React component 649→function SearchComponent() { 650→ const [query, setQuery] = useState(''); 651→ 652→ useEffect(() => { 653→ window.addEventListener('resize', handleResize); 654→ document.addEventListener('keydown', handleKeyPress); 655→ // No cleanup! 656→ }, []); 657→ 658→ // ... 659→} 660→``` 661→ 662→**Good code:** 663→```typescript 664→function SearchComponent() { 665→ const [query, setQuery] = useState(''); 666→ 667→ useEffect(() => { 668→ const handleResize = () => { /* ... */ }; 669→ const handleKeyPress = (e: KeyboardEvent) => { /* ... */ }; 670→ 671→ window.addEventListener('resize', handleResize); 672→ document.addEventListener('keydown', handleKeyPress); 673→ 674→ return () => { 675→ window.removeEventListener('resize', handleResize); 676→ document.removeEventListener('keydown', handleKeyPress); 677→ }; 678→ }, []); 679→ 680→ // ... 681→} 682→``` 683→ 684→**Why AI does this:** Setup code is more prominent in training data than cleanup code. AI focuses on making features work, not on lifecycle management. 685→ 686→**Impact:** Memory leaks causing progressive performance degradation, crashes in long-running applications. 687→ 688→**Detection:** React `eslint-plugin-react-hooks`. Chrome DevTools memory profiling. `useEffect` dependency exhaustive-deps rule. 689→ 690→**Sources:** 691→- [Memory Leaks in React TypeScript](https://medium.com/@surenpoghosian/how-to-prevent-memory-leaks-in-react-typescript-b48d10edd8dc) 692→ 693→--- 694→ 695→### 4.3 N+1 Query Problems 696→ 697→**Description:** AI generates code that makes individual database queries inside loops rather than batching. 698→ 699→**Bad code:** 700→```typescript 701→async function getUsersWithPosts(userIds: number[]) { 702→ const results = []; 703→ for (const id of userIds) { 704→ const user = await db.users.findOne({ id }); 705→ const posts = await db.posts.find({ userId: id }); // N+1! 706→ results.push({ ...user, posts }); 707→ } 708→ return results; 709→} 710→``` 711→ 712→**Good code:** 713→```typescript 714→async function getUsersWithPosts(userIds: number[]) { 715→ const users = await db.users.findMany({ 716→ where: { id: { in: userIds } }, 717→ include: { posts: true }, 718→ }); 719→ return users; 720→} 721→``` 722→ 723→**Why AI does this:** Iterative patterns are simpler to generate. AI doesn't reason about database round-trips. 724→ 725→**Impact:** Severe performance degradation. Each additional user adds a database round-trip. 726→ 727→**Detection:** ORM query logging. DataLoader pattern. Code review for `await` inside loops. 728→ 729→**Sources:** 730→- [AI-Assisted Development in 2026](https://dev.to/austinwdigital/ai-assisted-development-in-2026-best-practices-real-risks-and-the-new-bar-for-engineers-3fom) 731→ 732→--- 733→ 734→### 4.4 Unnecessary Over-Abstraction 735→ 736→**Description:** AI creates excessive layers of abstraction -- wrapper functions, factory patterns, and abstract classes where simple direct code would suffice. 737→ 738→**Bad code:** 739→```typescript 740→// AI generates enterprise-level abstraction for a simple task 741→interface ILoggerFactory { 742→ createLogger(name: string): ILogger; 743→} 744→ 745→interface ILogger { 746→ log(message: string): void; 747→} 748→ 749→class ConsoleLoggerFactory implements ILoggerFactory { 750→ createLogger(name: string): ILogger { 751→ return new ConsoleLogger(name); 752→ } 753→} 754→ 755→class ConsoleLogger implements ILogger { 756→ constructor(private name: string) {} 757→ log(message: string): void { 758→ console.log(`[${this.name}] ${message}`); 759→ } 760→} 761→ 762→// Usage 763→const factory = new ConsoleLoggerFactory(); 764→const logger = factory.createLogger('app'); 765→logger.log('Hello'); 766→``` 767→ 768→**Good code:** 769→```typescript 770→function log(name: string, message: string): void { 771→ console.log(`[${name}] ${message}`); 772→} 773→ 774→log('app', 'Hello'); 775→``` 776→ 777→**Why AI does this:** Training data includes enterprise Java/C# patterns. AI applies them indiscriminately regardless of project scale. 778→ 779→**Impact:** Cognitive complexity. "Dozens of micro-abstractions without clear ownership." Harder to understand and maintain. 780→ 781→**Detection:** Cognitive complexity metrics. Code review. Cyclomatic complexity maximum of 10. 782→ 783→--- 784→ 785→## 5. PACKAGE AND DEPENDENCY ISSUES 786→ 787→### 5.1 Hallucinated Packages (Slopsquatting) 788→ 789→**Description:** AI confidently recommends npm packages that do not exist. In ~20% of examined cases (across 576,000 samples), recommended packages didn't exist. Attackers can register these hallucinated package names with malicious code ("slopsquatting"). 790→ 791→**Bad code:** 792→```typescript 793→import { validateEmail } from 'email-validator-pro'; // Doesn't exist 794→import { utilityFunction } from 'lodash-advanced'; // Doesn't exist 795→import { parse } from 'ts-migrate-parser'; // Hallucinated by ChatGPT 796→``` 797→ 798→**Good code:** 799→```typescript 800→import validator from 'validator'; // Real, well-known package 801→import { merge } from 'lodash'; // Real, well-known package 802→import ts from 'typescript'; // Real, official package 803→``` 804→ 805→**Why AI does this:** "A majority of hallucinations are not just random noise, but repeatable artifacts of how the models respond to certain prompts." Models generate plausible names based on naming conventions. 806→ 807→**Impact:** Supply chain attacks if an attacker registers the hallucinated package name. Build failures. Wasted developer time. 808→ 809→**Detection:** `npm install` will fail for non-existent packages. Verify all packages in npm registry before installation. Use lockfiles. ESLint `import/no-unresolved`. 810→ 811→**Sources:** 812→- [Slopsquatting: AI Hallucinations and Supply Chain Risk](https://fossa.com/blog/slopsquatting-ai-hallucinations-new-software-supply-chain-risk/) 813→- [AI Code Tools Widely Hallucinate Packages](https://www.darkreading.com/application-security/ai-code-tools-widely-hallucinate-packages) 814→- [AI-Hallucinated Code Dependencies](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/ai-hallucinated-code-dependencies-become-new-supply-chain-risk/) 815→- [Package Hallucinations (Snyk)](https://snyk.io/articles/package-hallucinations/) 816→ 817→--- 818→ 819→### 5.2 Outdated/Deprecated Library APIs 820→ 821→**Description:** AI training data contains deprecated packages and old API patterns. It recommends libraries that were archived years ago. 822→ 823→**Bad code:** 824→```typescript 825→// 'request' was deprecated/archived in 2020 826→import * as request from 'request'; 827→request.get(url, callback); 828→ 829→// Old Express error handling 830→app.use((err, req, res, next) => { 831→ res.status(500).send(err.message); // Leaks error details 832→}); 833→ 834→// moment.js (legacy, 300KB+) 835→import moment from 'moment'; 836→const date = moment().format('YYYY-MM-DD'); 837→``` 838→ 839→**Good code:** 840→```typescript 841→// Modern alternatives 842→const response = await fetch(url); 843→ 844→// Or with error handling 845→import { ofetch } from 'ofetch'; 846→const data = await ofetch(url); 847→ 848→// date-fns (tree-shakeable, modern) 849→import { format } from 'date-fns'; 850→const date = format(new Date(), 'yyyy-MM-dd'); 851→``` 852→ 853→**Why AI does this:** Training data cutoff and frequency bias. Older libraries appear more frequently in training data. 854→ 855→**Impact:** Known vulnerabilities (CVEs), missing security patches, larger bundle sizes, no maintenance. 856→ 857→**Detection:** `npm audit`. Check package last-published dates (should be recent). `npm outdated`. Dependency scanning in CI. 858→ 859→**Sources:** 860→- [Debugging AI-Generated Code](https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/debugging-ai-generated-code-8-failure-patterns-and-fixes) 861→ 862→--- 863→ 864→### 5.3 Excessive Dependencies 865→ 866→**Description:** AI pulls in heavy dependencies for trivial tasks. A simple to-do list app generated by AI included 2-5 backend dependencies that weren't needed. 867→ 868→**Bad code:** 869→```typescript 870→import _ from 'lodash'; // 72KB for one function 871→ 872→const unique = _.uniq(array); 873→``` 874→ 875→**Good code:** 876→```typescript 877→const unique = [...new Set(array)]; 878→``` 879→ 880→**Why AI does this:** Training data frequently imports popular libraries. AI doesn't evaluate whether a native alternative exists. 881→ 882→**Impact:** Larger bundle size, more attack surface, more maintenance burden, slower builds. 883→ 884→**Detection:** Bundle analysis (webpack-bundle-analyzer). Dependency review. `you-dont-need-lodash` patterns. 885→ 886→--- 887→ 888→## 6. ERROR HANDLING PROBLEMS 889→ 890→### 6.1 Swallowed Errors (Empty Catch Blocks) 891→ 892→**Description:** AI wraps code in try/catch but does nothing meaningful with the error, silently hiding failures. 893→ 894→**Bad code:** 895→```typescript 896→async function fetchData(url: string) { 897→ try { 898→ const response = await fetch(url); 899→ return response.json(); 900→ } catch (e) { 901→ console.log(e); // Swallowed - caller doesn't know it failed 902→ } 903→} 904→ 905→// Or worse: 906→try { 907→ await riskyOperation(); 908→} catch (e) { 909→ // empty 910→} 911→``` 912→ 913→**Good code:** 914→```typescript 915→async function fetchData(url: string): Promise { 916→ try { 917→ const response = await fetch(url); 918→ if (!response.ok) { 919→ throw new HttpError(response.status, `HTTP ${response.status}: ${response.statusText}`); 920→ } 921→ return response.json(); 922→ } catch (error) { 923→ if (error instanceof HttpError) { 924→ throw error; 925→ } 926→ throw new NetworkError('Failed to fetch data', { cause: error }); 927→ } 928→} 929→``` 930→ 931→**Why AI does this:** AI adds try/catch because it's the expected pattern, but doesn't reason about error propagation or recovery strategies. 932→ 933→**Impact:** Silent failures in production. Difficult debugging. Data corruption from partial operations. 934→ 935→**Detection:** ESLint `no-empty` and `@typescript-eslint/no-empty-function`. Code review for catch blocks. 936→ 937→--- 938→ 939→### 6.2 Information Leakage in Error Messages 940→ 941→**Description:** AI exposes internal system details, stack traces, and database schemas in error responses. 942→ 943→**Bad code:** 944→```typescript 945→app.use((err: Error, req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) => { 946→ res.status(500).json({ 947→ error: err.message, 948→ stack: err.stack, // Exposes internal details! 949→ query: req.query, // Exposes user input 950→ }); 951→}); 952→``` 953→ 954→**Good code:** 955→```typescript 956→app.use((err: Error, req: Request, res: Response, next: NextFunction) => { 957→ const errorId = crypto.randomUUID(); 958→ logger.error('Request failed', { errorId, error: err, path: req.path }); 959→ 960→ res.status(500).json({ 961→ error: 'Internal server error', 962→ errorId, // For support correlation 963→ }); 964→}); 965→``` 966→ 967→**Why AI does this:** Training data includes development-mode error handlers. AI doesn't distinguish between dev and production contexts. 968→ 969→**Impact:** Information disclosure. Attackers learn about internal architecture, database schemas, file paths. 970→ 971→**Detection:** Security testing. Penetration testing. SAST rules for error exposure. 972→ 973→**Sources:** 974→- [OpenSSF Security Guide](https://best.openssf.org/Security-Focused-Guide-for-AI-Code-Assistant-Instructions) 975→ 976→--- 977→ 978→### 6.3 Catching `unknown` as Typed Error 979→ 980→**Description:** In TypeScript strict mode, caught errors are `unknown`, but AI treats them as `Error` objects. 981→ 982→**Bad code:** 983→```typescript 984→try { 985→ await someOperation(); 986→} catch (error) { 987→ console.log(error.message); // Error: 'error' is of type 'unknown' 988→ console.log(error.stack); 989→} 990→``` 991→ 992→**Good code:** 993→```typescript 994→try { 995→ await someOperation(); 996→} catch (error) { 997→ if (error instanceof Error) { 998→ console.error(error.message); 999→ } else { 1000→ console.error('Unknown error:', String(error)); 1001→ } 1002→} 1003→``` 1004→ 1005→**Why AI does this:** Many training examples predate TypeScript's `useUnknownInCatchVariables` (TS 4.4+). Older patterns treat `catch (error)` as implicitly typed `Error`. 1006→ 1007→**Impact:** TypeScript compile errors in strict mode. Runtime errors if a non-Error value is thrown. 1008→ 1009→**Detection:** TypeScript `useUnknownInCatchVariables: true` (included in `strict`). 1010→ 1011→--- 1012→ 1013→## 7. ASYNC/AWAIT AND PROMISE MISTAKES 1014→ 1015→### 7.1 Floating Promises (Forgotten Await) 1016→ 1017→**Description:** AI calls async functions without `await`, creating floating promises whose rejections go unhandled. 1018→ 1019→**Bad code:** 1020→```typescript 1021→function saveAndNotify(user: User) { 1022→ saveToDatabase(user); // Returns Promise, not awaited! 1023→ sendNotification(user.email); // Returns Promise, not awaited! 1024→ console.log('Done'); // Runs before save/notify complete 1025→} 1026→ 1027→// In Express route 1028→app.post('/users', (req, res) => { 1029→ createUser(req.body); // Floating promise 1030→ res.json({ success: true }); // Responds before user is created 1031→}); 1032→``` 1033→ 1034→**Good code:** 1035→```typescript 1036→async function saveAndNotify(user: User): Promise { 1037→ await saveToDatabase(user); 1038→ await sendNotification(user.email); 1039→ console.log('Done'); 1040→} 1041→ 1042→// In Express route 1043→app.post('/users', async (req, res) => { 1044→ try { 1045→ await createUser(req.body); 1046→ res.json({ success: true }); 1047→ } catch (error) { 1048→ res.status(500).json({ error: 'Failed to create user' }); 1049→ } 1050→}); 1051→``` 1052→ 1053→**Why AI does this:** AI sometimes generates function calls without recognizing they return promises. The pattern looks correct syntactically. 1054→ 1055→**Impact:** Race conditions. Unhandled promise rejections (crash in Node.js). Data inconsistency. 1056→ 1057→**Detection:** ESLint `@typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises` (critical rule). `@typescript-eslint/no-misused-promises`. 1058→ 1059→**Sources:** 1060→- [no-floating-promises rule](https://typescript-eslint.io/rules/no-floating-promises/) 1061→- [no-misused-promises rule](https://typescript-eslint.io/rules/no-misused-promises/) 1062→ 1063→--- 1064→ 1065→### 7.2 Sequential Awaits Where Parallel Is Appropriate 1066→ 1067→**Description:** AI awaits promises sequentially when they could run in parallel, creating unnecessary latency. 1068→ 1069→**Bad code:** 1070→```typescript 1071→async function fetchDashboard(userId: string) { 1072→ const user = await fetchUser(userId); // 200ms 1073→ const posts = await fetchPosts(userId); // 300ms 1074→ const notifications = await fetchNotifications(userId); // 150ms 1075→ // Total: 650ms sequential 1076→ return { user, posts, notifications }; 1077→} 1078→``` 1079→ 1080→**Good code:** 1081→```typescript 1082→async function fetchDashboard(userId: string) { 1083→ const [user, posts, notifications] = await Promise.all([ 1084→ fetchUser(userId), 1085→ fetchPosts(userId), 1086→ fetchNotifications(userId), 1087→ ]); 1088→ // Total: ~300ms parallel 1089→ return { user, posts, notifications }; 1090→} 1091→``` 1092→ 1093→**Why AI does this:** Sequential await is the simpler, more common pattern in training data. AI doesn't analyze data dependencies. 1094→ 1095→**Impact:** 2-5x slower response times for operations that could be parallelized. 1096→ 1097→**Detection:** Code review for consecutive independent `await` calls. Performance monitoring. 1098→ 1099→--- 1100→ 1101→### 7.3 Promises Passed Where Callbacks or Booleans Are Expected 1102→ 1103→**Description:** AI passes async functions to APIs that expect synchronous callbacks, such as `Array.forEach`, or uses promises in boolean contexts. 1104→ 1105→**Bad code:** 1106→```typescript 1107→// forEach doesn't await - all promises fire simultaneously with no error handling 1108→users.forEach(async (user) => { 1109→ await sendEmail(user.email); 1110→}); 1111→console.log('All emails sent'); // Runs immediately, not after sends 1112→ 1113→// Promise in boolean context (always truthy) 1114→if (fetchUser(id)) { // This is a Promise, always truthy! 1115→ console.log('User exists'); 1116→} 1117→``` 1118→ 1119→**Good code:** 1120→```typescript 1121→// Use for...of for sequential 1122→for (const user of users) { 1123→ await sendEmail(user.email); 1124→} 1125→ 1126→// Or Promise.all for parallel 1127→await Promise.all(users.map(user => sendEmail(user.email))); 1128→ 1129→// Await the promise 1130→const user = await fetchUser(id); 1131→if (user) { 1132→ console.log('User exists'); 1133→} 1134→``` 1135→ 1136→**Why AI does this:** `forEach` is extremely common in training data. AI doesn't distinguish between sync and async iteration contexts. 1137→ 1138→**Impact:** Silent failures. Race conditions. Logic errors from truthy promise objects. 1139→ 1140→**Detection:** ESLint `@typescript-eslint/no-misused-promises`. `no-return-await` rules. 1141→ 1142→--- 1143→ 1144→### 7.4 Missing Error Handling in Promise Chains 1145→ 1146→**Description:** AI creates promise chains or `Promise.all` calls without handling rejections. 1147→ 1148→**Bad code:** 1149→```typescript 1150→async function fetchAllData(urls: string[]) { 1151→ const results = await Promise.all( 1152→ urls.map(url => fetch(url).then(r => r.json())) 1153→ ); 1154→ // If ANY fetch fails, ALL results are lost 1155→ return results; 1156→} 1157→``` 1158→ 1159→**Good code:** 1160→```typescript 1161→async function fetchAllData(urls: string[]) { 1162→ const results = await Promise.allSettled( 1163→ urls.map(url => fetch(url).then(r => r.json())) 1164→ ); 1165→ 1166→ return results.map((result, i) => { 1167→ if (result.status === 'fulfilled') { 1168→ return { url: urls[i], data: result.value, error: null }; 1169→ } 1170→ return { url: urls[i], data: null, error: result.reason }; 1171→ }); 1172→} 1173→``` 1174→ 1175→**Why AI does this:** `Promise.all` is the more common pattern. `Promise.allSettled` is newer and less represented in training data. 1176→ 1177→**Impact:** One failure causes total data loss from all parallel requests. 1178→ 1179→**Detection:** Code review for `Promise.all` with external calls. Consider `Promise.allSettled` for fault tolerance. 1180→ 1181→--- 1182→ 1183→## 8. REACT / NEXT.JS SPECIFIC ISSUES 1184→ 1185→### 8.1 Unnecessary useEffect for Derived State 1186→ 1187→**Description:** AI uses `useState` + `useEffect` to compute values that could be calculated during render. This is one of the most common React anti-patterns AI produces. 1188→ 1189→**Bad code:** 1190→```typescript 1191→function ProductList({ products }: { products: Product[] }) { 1192→ const [filteredProducts, setFilteredProducts] = useState([]); 1193→ const [totalPrice, setTotalPrice] = useState(0); 1194→ 1195→ useEffect(() => { 1196→ const filtered = products.filter(p => p.inStock); 1197→ setFilteredProducts(filtered); 1198→ setTotalPrice(filtered.reduce((sum, p) => sum + p.price, 0)); 1199→ }, [products]); 1200→ 1201→ return
{/* ... */}
; 1202→} 1203→``` 1204→ 1205→**Good code:** 1206→```typescript 1207→function ProductList({ products }: { products: Product[] }) { 1208→ const filteredProducts = products.filter(p => p.inStock); 1209→ const totalPrice = filteredProducts.reduce((sum, p) => sum + p.price, 0); 1210→ 1211→ // If expensive, use useMemo 1212→ // const filteredProducts = useMemo( 1213→ // () => products.filter(p => p.inStock), 1214→ // [products] 1215→ // ); 1216→ 1217→ return
{/* ... */}
; 1218→} 1219→``` 1220→ 1221→**Why AI does this:** "useState and useEffect are antipatterns" for synchronous derived data, but they're the most common hooks in training data. AI defaults to what it sees most often. 1222→ 1223→**Impact:** Unnecessary re-renders (renders once with stale data, then again with computed data). Flash of incorrect content. Performance degradation. 1224→ 1225→**Detection:** React DevTools profiler. ESLint `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps`. The official React docs: "You Might Not Need an Effect." 1226→ 1227→**Sources:** 1228→- [You Might Not Need an Effect (React docs)](https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect) 1229→- [Common React Anti-Patterns](https://oozou.com/blog/6-react-anti-patterns-to-avoid-206) 1230→- [Why AI-Generated React and Next.js Code Breaks](https://medium.com/@prakashjhandls/why-ai-generated-react-and-next-js-code-breaks-and-how-i-fix-it-quickly-efc8c5fd587a) 1231→ 1232→--- 1233→ 1234→### 8.2 Missing useEffect Cleanup 1235→ 1236→**Description:** AI adds subscriptions, timers, and event listeners in useEffect without returning a cleanup function. 1237→ 1238→**Bad code:** 1239→```typescript 1240→useEffect(() => { 1241→ const interval = setInterval(pollApi, 5000); 1242→ const ws = new WebSocket('ws://api.example.com'); 1243→ ws.onmessage = (event) => setData(JSON.parse(event.data)); 1244→ // No cleanup! Memory leak + stale updates 1245→}, []); 1246→``` 1247→ 1248→**Good code:** 1249→```typescript 1250→useEffect(() => { 1251→ const interval = setInterval(pollApi, 5000); 1252→ const ws = new WebSocket('ws://api.example.com'); 1253→ ws.onmessage = (event) => setData(JSON.parse(event.data)); 1254→ 1255→ return () => { 1256→ clearInterval(interval); 1257→ ws.close(); 1258→ }; 1259→}, []); 1260→``` 1261→ 1262→**Why AI does this:** Cleanup logic is less prominent in training data. AI focuses on the "setup" side of effects. 1263→ 1264→**Impact:** Memory leaks, stale state updates after unmount, "Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component" warnings. 1265→ 1266→**Detection:** `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` exhaustive-deps rule. React StrictMode (double-invokes effects in development). 1267→ 1268→--- 1269→ 1270→### 8.3 Incorrect useEffect Dependency Arrays 1271→ 1272→**Description:** AI either omits dependencies (causing stale closures) or includes too many (causing infinite re-render loops). 1273→ 1274→**Bad code (missing deps):** 1275→```typescript 1276→function SearchResults({ query }: { query: string }) { 1277→ const [results, setResults] = useState([]); 1278→ 1279→ useEffect(() => { 1280→ fetchResults(query).then(setResults); 1281→ }, []); // Missing 'query' dependency - stale closure! 1282→ 1283→ return
{results.map(/* ... */)}
; 1284→} 1285→``` 1286→ 1287→**Bad code (infinite loop):** 1288→```typescript 1289→function UserProfile({ userId }: { userId: string }) { 1290→ const [user, setUser] = useState(null); 1291→ const options = { include: 'posts' }; // New object every render 1292→ 1293→ useEffect(() => { 1294→ fetchUser(userId, options).then(setUser); 1295→ }, [userId, options]); // options changes every render = infinite loop 1296→``` 1297→ 1298→**Good code:** 1299→```typescript 1300→function SearchResults({ query }: { query: string }) { 1301→ const [results, setResults] = useState([]); 1302→ 1303→ useEffect(() => { 1304→ let cancelled = false; 1305→ fetchResults(query).then((data) => { 1306→ if (!cancelled) setResults(data); 1307→ }); 1308→ return () => { cancelled = true; }; 1309→ }, [query]); 1310→ 1311→ return
{results.map(/* ... */)}
; 1312→} 1313→``` 1314→ 1315→**Why AI does this:** Dependency arrays require understanding the closure semantics of JavaScript, which AI models struggle with. 1316→ 1317→**Detection:** `react-hooks/exhaustive-deps` ESLint rule. React StrictMode. 1318→ 1319→--- 1320→ 1321→### 8.4 Hallucinated Next.js Routes and APIs 1322→ 1323→**Description:** AI generates Next.js API routes, middleware, or page paths that don't exist in the project structure or don't match the framework version (Pages Router vs. App Router). 1324→ 1325→**Bad code:** 1326→```typescript 1327→// AI mixes App Router and Pages Router patterns 1328→// In app/api/users/route.ts (App Router) 1329→export default function handler(req, res) { // Pages Router pattern! 1330→ res.status(200).json({ users: [] }); 1331→} 1332→``` 1333→ 1334→**Good code:** 1335→```typescript 1336→// App Router pattern (app/api/users/route.ts) 1337→import { NextResponse } from 'next/server'; 1338→ 1339→export async function GET() { 1340→ return NextResponse.json({ users: [] }); 1341→} 1342→ 1343→// Pages Router pattern (pages/api/users.ts) 1344→import type { NextApiRequest, NextApiResponse } from 'next'; 1345→ 1346→export default function handler(req: NextApiRequest, res: NextApiResponse) { 1347→ res.status(200).json({ users: [] }); 1348→} 1349→``` 1350→ 1351→**Why AI does this:** Next.js has had multiple major routing paradigms. Training data contains both, and AI conflates them. 1352→ 1353→**Impact:** Broken routes, 404 errors, incorrect request handling. 1354→ 1355→**Detection:** Next.js build errors. Integration tests. Verify route file locations match the router pattern. 1356→ 1357→**Sources:** 1358→- [Why AI-Generated React and Next.js Code Breaks](https://medium.com/@prakashjhandls/why-ai-generated-react-and-next-js-code-breaks-and-how-i-fix-it-quickly-efc8c5fd587a) 1359→ 1360→--- 1361→ 1362→### 8.5 Client/Server Boundary Confusion 1363→ 1364→**Description:** AI uses browser APIs in server components or server-only APIs in client components. 1365→ 1366→**Bad code:** 1367→```typescript 1368→// app/components/UserList.tsx (Server Component by default in App Router) 1369→export default function UserList() { 1370→ const [users, setUsers] = useState([]); // useState in server component! 1371→ // ... 1372→} 1373→``` 1374→ 1375→**Good code:** 1376→```typescript 1377→// app/components/UserList.tsx (Server Component) 1378→export default async function UserList() { 1379→ const users = await db.users.findMany(); 1380→ return
    {users.map(u =>
  • {u.name}
    • )}
; 1381→} 1382→ 1383→// app/components/UserSearch.tsx (Client Component) 1384→'use client'; 1385→import { useState } from 'react'; 1386→ 1387→export default function UserSearch() { 1388→ const [query, setQuery] = useState(''); 1389→ // ... 1390→} 1391→``` 1392→ 1393→**Why AI does this:** The App Router server/client boundary is relatively new. Training data heavily favors the older client-side-only React patterns. 1394→ 1395→**Impact:** Build errors. Hydration mismatches. Leaked secrets in client bundles if server code runs on client. 1396→ 1397→--- 1398→ 1399→## 9. TESTING ANTI-PATTERNS 1400→ 1401→### 9.1 Over-Mocking (Testing Nothing Real) 1402→ 1403→**Description:** AI mocks every dependency so heavily that tests only verify mocked behavior, not actual application logic. Coverage appears high while catching zero real bugs. 1404→ 1405→**Bad code:** 1406→```typescript 1407→// Tests mock everything - tests nothing real 1408→jest.mock('./database'); 1409→jest.mock('./emailService'); 1410→jest.mock('./logger'); 1411→jest.mock('./validator'); 1412→ 1413→test('createUser creates a user', async () => { 1414→ (database.save as jest.Mock).mockResolvedValue({ id: 1 }); 1415→ (validator.validate as jest.Mock).mockReturnValue(true); 1416→ 1417→ const result = await createUser({ name: 'Test' }); 1418→ 1419→ expect(database.save).toHaveBeenCalled(); // Only verifies the mock was called 1420→ expect(result).toEqual({ id: 1 }); // Only verifies mock return value 1421→}); 1422→``` 1423→ 1424→**Good code:** 1425→```typescript 1426→// Integration test with real (test) database 1427→test('createUser creates a user with valid data', async () => { 1428→ const user = await createUser({ 1429→ name: 'Test User', 1430→ email: 'test@example.com', 1431→ }); 1432→ 1433→ expect(user.id).toBeDefined(); 1434→ expect(user.name).toBe('Test User'); 1435→ 1436→ // Verify in database 1437→ const saved = await db.users.findUnique({ where: { id: user.id } }); 1438→ expect(saved).not.toBeNull(); 1439→ expect(saved?.email).toBe('test@example.com'); 1440→}); 1441→ 1442→test('createUser rejects invalid email', async () => { 1443→ await expect( 1444→ createUser({ name: 'Test', email: 'not-an-email' }) 1445→ ).rejects.toThrow('Invalid email'); 1446→}); 1447→``` 1448→ 1449→**Why AI does this:** "AI agents mock so heavily they test nothing real." AI produces surface-level correctness. Mocking is the most common pattern in training data for test files. 1450→ 1451→**Impact:** "A test suite with 100% coverage but 4% mutation score executes every line and misses 96% of potential bugs." 1452→ 1453→**Detection:** Mutation testing (Stryker). Review mock count per test. Integration tests should outnumber unit tests for service logic. 1454→ 1455→**Sources:** 1456→- [How to Test AI-Generated Code the Right Way](https://www.twocents.software/blog/how-to-test-ai-generated-code-the-right-way/) 1457→- [AI vs Human Code Generation Report](https://www.coderabbit.ai/blog/state-of-ai-vs-human-code-generation-report) 1458→ 1459→--- 1460→ 1461→### 9.2 Tests That Test AI's Assumptions, Not Requirements 1462→ 1463→**Description:** AI generates tests that validate its own implementation rather than the actual business requirements. 1464→ 1465→**Bad code:** 1466→```typescript 1467→// AI tests its own implementation detail 1468→test('formatPrice returns formatted price', () => { 1469→ expect(formatPrice(10)).toBe('$10.00'); // What if locale is EUR? 1470→}); 1471→ 1472→test('getDiscount calculates discount', () => { 1473→ expect(getDiscount(100, 10)).toBe(90); // Does this match the business rule? 1474→ // Missing: volume discounts, membership tiers, minimum order, etc. 1475→}); 1476→``` 1477→ 1478→**Good code:** 1479→```typescript 1480→// Tests based on business requirements 1481→describe('formatPrice', () => { 1482→ test('formats USD price with 2 decimal places', () => { 1483→ expect(formatPrice(10, 'USD')).toBe('$10.00'); 1484→ }); 1485→ 1486→ test('formats EUR price with European convention', () => { 1487→ expect(formatPrice(10, 'EUR')).toBe('10,00 EUR'); 1488→ }); 1489→ 1490→ test('handles zero price', () => { 1491→ expect(formatPrice(0, 'USD')).toBe('$0.00'); 1492→ }); 1493→ 1494→ test('rounds to nearest cent', () => { 1495→ expect(formatPrice(10.999, 'USD')).toBe('$11.00'); 1496→ }); 1497→}); 1498→``` 1499→ 1500→**Why AI does this:** AI "tests its own assumptions rather than actual intent" and "rarely includes complex edge cases, domain-specific constraints, or legacy system integration needs." 1501→ 1502→**Impact:** False confidence. Tests pass but don't catch regressions in business logic. 1503→ 1504→**Detection:** Review tests against requirements docs. Mutation testing. Coverage of edge cases. 1505→ 1506→--- 1507→ 1508→### 9.3 Snapshot Test Overuse 1509→ 1510→**Description:** AI generates snapshot tests for components that change frequently, leading to meaningless snapshot updates that are rubber-stamped. 1511→ 1512→**Bad code:** 1513→```typescript 1514→test('renders UserDashboard', () => { 1515→ const { container } = render(); 1516→ expect(container).toMatchSnapshot(); // 500-line snapshot that changes with every UI tweak 1517→}); 1518→``` 1519→ 1520→**Good code:** 1521→```typescript 1522→test('displays user name and email', () => { 1523→ render(); 1524→ expect(screen.getByText('John Doe')).toBeInTheDocument(); 1525→ expect(screen.getByText('john@example.com')).toBeInTheDocument(); 1526→}); 1527→ 1528→test('shows edit button for authenticated users', () => { 1529→ render(); 1530→ expect(screen.getByRole('button', { name: /edit profile/i })).toBeInTheDocument(); 1531→}); 1532→``` 1533→ 1534→**Why AI does this:** Snapshot tests are the simplest way to "test" a component. AI optimizes for ease of generation. 1535→ 1536→**Impact:** Snapshot fatigue. Developers blindly update snapshots without reviewing changes. 1537→ 1538→**Detection:** Limit snapshot size. Prefer targeted assertions with Testing Library. Code review for snapshot tests. 1539→ 1540→--- 1541→ 1542→## 10. ARCHITECTURAL AND STRUCTURAL ANTI-PATTERNS 1543→ 1544→### 10.1 Monolithic File Structure 1545→ 1546→**Description:** AI generates large, monolithic files with tightly coupled components instead of modular architecture. 1547→ 1548→**Why AI does this:** Context window limitations mean AI works file-by-file. "AI coding agents never suggest refactoring." This leads to duplicated logic across files and inconsistent patterns. 1549→ 1550→**Impact:** Cognitive complexity scores of 20+ (recommended max: 15). Difficult to test, review, and maintain. 1551→ 1552→**Detection:** Cognitive complexity analysis. File length limits. Module coupling metrics. 1553→ 1554→**Sources:** 1555→- [Understanding Anti-Patterns in AI-Generated Code](https://www.softwareseni.com/understanding-anti-patterns-and-quality-degradation-in-ai-generated-code/) 1556→ 1557→--- 1558→ 1559→### 10.2 Excessive Comments 1560→ 1561→**Description:** AI generates inline comments for every line, increasing noise without adding value. 1562→ 1563→**Bad code:** 1564→```typescript 1565→// Get the user from the database 1566→const user = await db.users.findOne({ id: userId }); 1567→// Check if the user exists 1568→if (!user) { 1569→ // Return a 404 error 1570→ return res.status(404).json({ error: 'User not found' }); 1571→} 1572→// Get the user's email 1573→const email = user.email; 1574→// Convert to lowercase 1575→const normalizedEmail = email.toLowerCase(); 1576→``` 1577→ 1578→**Good code:** 1579→```typescript 1580→const user = await db.users.findOne({ id: userId }); 1581→if (!user) { 1582→ return res.status(404).json({ error: 'User not found' }); 1583→} 1584→const normalizedEmail = user.email.toLowerCase(); 1585→``` 1586→ 1587→**Why AI does this:** This is the #1 most common AI anti-pattern, occurring in 90-100% of generated code. AI is trained to be helpful and explanatory. 1588→ 1589→**Impact:** Cognitive overload. Comments become stale and misleading. Doubles file size for no value. 1590→ 1591→**Sources:** 1592→- [Understanding Anti-Patterns in AI-Generated Code](https://www.softwareseni.com/understanding-anti-patterns-and-quality-degradation-in-ai-generated-code/) 1593→ 1594→--- 1595→ 1596→### 10.3 Duplicated Logic Across Files (Context Blindness) 1597→ 1598→**Description:** AI generates the same utility function or business logic in multiple places because it cannot see the full codebase. 1599→ 1600→**Why AI does this:** Context window limitations. Copilot sees one file at a time. Even Cursor/Claude Code with 200K token contexts cannot see entire large codebases. 1601→ 1602→**Impact:** Inconsistent behavior when one copy is updated but others aren't. Maintenance burden. 1603→ 1604→**Detection:** Code duplication analysis (jscpd, SonarQube). File-level search for similar function names. 1605→ 1606→--- 1607→ 1608→### 10.4 Missing Environment Validation 1609→ 1610→**Description:** AI uses environment variables without validation, causing cryptic runtime failures in deployment. 1611→ 1612→**Bad code:** 1613→```typescript 1614→const apiKey = process.env.API_KEY; // Could be undefined 1615→const client = new ApiClient(apiKey); // Passes undefined 1616→``` 1617→ 1618→**Good code:** 1619→```typescript 1620→import { z } from 'zod'; 1621→ 1622→const envSchema = z.object({ 1623→ API_KEY: z.string().min(1), 1624→ DATABASE_URL: z.string().url(), 1625→ NODE_ENV: z.enum(['development', 'production', 'test']), 1626→}); 1627→ 1628→const env = envSchema.parse(process.env); 1629→// Now env.API_KEY is guaranteed to be a non-empty string 1630→``` 1631→ 1632→**Sources:** 1633→- [Debugging AI-Generated Code](https://www.augmentcode.com/guides/debugging-ai-generated-code-8-failure-patterns-and-fixes) 1634→ 1635→--- 1636→ 1637→## KEY STATISTICS 1638→ 1639→- AI-authored PRs produce **10.83 issues per PR** vs **6.45 for human-only PRs** (1.4-1.7x more critical/major findings) 1640→- **45% of AI-generated code** contains security vulnerabilities 1641→- **~20% of AI-recommended packages** don't exist 1642→- **60% of faults** in AI code are silent logic failures 1643→- GPT-4 generated code runs **3x slower** than human solutions      1→     1→# Comprehensive Research: AI-Generated Python Code Flaws, Mistakes, and Anti-Patterns
     2→     2→
     3→     3→## Executive Summary
     4→     4→
     5→     5→Research across academic papers, industry reports, and developer communities reveals that **40-45% of AI-generated code contains vulnerabilities**, AI-generated PRs contain **1.7x more issues** than human-written code (~10.83 vs 6.45 issues per PR), and **silent logic failures make up 60% of faults**. Python is the most-studied language in this space (61% of all studies), making the findings especially robust.
     6→     6→
     7→     7→---
     8→     8→
     9→     9→## 1. SECURITY VULNERABILITIES
    10→    10→
    11→    11→### 1.1 SQL Injection (CWE-89)
    12→    12→
    13→    13→**Description:** AI models frequently generate SQL queries using string concatenation or f-strings instead of parameterized queries. This is the single most reproduced security flaw across all AI coding assistants.
    14→    14→
    15→    15→**Bad Code:**
    16→    16→```python
    17→    17→# AI-generated vulnerable code
    18→    18→def get_user(user_id):
    19→    19→    query = f"SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = {user_id}"
    20→    20→    cursor.execute(query)
    21→    21→    return cursor.fetchone()
    22→    22→
    23→    23→# Also vulnerable - % formatting
    24→    24→query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '%s'" % username
    25→    25→cursor.execute(query)
    26→    26→```
    27→    27→
    28→    28→**Good Code:**
    29→    29→```python
    30→    30→# Parameterized query - secure
    31→    31→def get_user(user_id):
    32→    32→    query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s"
    33→    33→    cursor.execute(query, (user_id,))

    35→    35→
